title: Getting Started with Unreal Engine 5
summary: My first steps into UE5 — setting up a project, learning the editor, and figuring out why my mesh was inside-out.
content:
It started with a blank project and a lot of confusion. UE5's Lumen and Nanite are genuinely impressive once you get past the initial setup. First lesson: always check your normals.

I spent the first week just learning the viewport controls and understanding how actors, components, and blueprints fit together. The Blueprint system is surprisingly powerful for prototyping before you drop into C++.

Highly recommend the official Lyra sample project as a reference — it's overwhelming at first but extremely well-structured.

title: C++ vs Blueprints: When to Use Which
summary: A breakdown of my workflow — when I reach for C++ and when Blueprints are just faster.
content:
Short answer: C++ for systems, Blueprints for iteration.

I write all my core game logic — movement, combat math, data structures — in C++. It's faster at runtime and easier to maintain long-term. But for quickly wiring up UI interactions or testing a mechanic idea, Blueprints let me move fast without recompiling.

The hybrid workflow is honestly the sweet spot. Expose your C++ functions to Blueprints with UFUNCTION(BlueprintCallable) and you get the best of both.

title: 3D Modeling for Games: What I Wish I Knew Earlier
summary: Hard-learned tips from months of modeling — topology, UV unwrapping, and why poly count actually matters.
content:
I learned Blender by breaking things. A lot of things.

The biggest early mistake: not thinking about topology. Beautiful high-poly sculpts are useless in-game if they don't deform properly or can't be retopologized cleanly. I now always model with deformation in mind, even for static props.

UV unwrapping is the unsexy part nobody warns you about. It's tedious, but a clean UV map is the difference between textures that look painted-on and textures that look real. Get comfortable with the UV editor early.
